title: Technological Change and Its Effects on Work
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to give examples of inventions or technological advances and explain how they changed how people work or live.
subject: social_studies
note: Thin shared ground. One member is a primary-grade generalization that technology changed daily life; the other is a list of industrial-era inventions and their effect on business productivity. They meet only at 'technology changes things,' an abstraction broad enough that many unrelated standards would pass this membership test. The reviewer marked the pair partial and said so. Weakest pair in the set after seed-026.

title: Individual Contributions That Shape History
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to study the lives or contributions of specific historical figures in order to see how individual actions shaped the world people live in now.
subject: social_studies

title: Respect for the Rights of Others
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain why people must respect others' rights and act responsibly toward the well-being of the group.
subject: social_studies

title: The Need for Rules and Laws
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain why groups and communities need rules and laws at all, and that such rules direct how people behave.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: 'Why groups and communities need rules and laws at all' and 'what laws accomplish for a community' are the same question answered at two vocabulary levels, and the K-2 framing is not a separate concept because concepts are grade-agnostic; the survivor's test is stronger because it enumerates the purposes (order, security, public services, protection of rights) and explicitly excludes standards that only assert laws exist (see report: close call).

title: How Rules Are Made and Why
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the process by which rules come to exist and the reasons behind them, rather than the content of any particular rule.
subject: social_studies
note: Partial pair. One member is about rules varying across settings; the other is about how rules are made and why. Neither states the other's idea — they meet only at the general proposition that rules exist for reasons. The title above follows the second member because that is the more discriminating half; a reviewer may prefer to reject the pair or re-anchor it to seed-004.

title: Goods and Services Produced by People's Work
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify or explain that people work at jobs to make goods or provide services that others use.
subject: social_studies

title: Money as a Medium of Exchange
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that people use money to buy and sell goods and services, or why money works better than direct barter.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: Both reduce to 'exchange runs on money rather than direct barter' — the survivor states it plainly and c-currency-enables-exchange-in-trade is that same proposition at a higher grade, as its own curator note already suspects; its extra cross-border currency-conversion clause is a genuinely different idea that belongs in its own concept, not as a reason to keep two barter-vs-money tests that both admit the same standards.

title: Income Earned Through Work
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that people earn income by working.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate concept: seed-008 and seed-009 state the same idea and carry identical title and description. Both pair the same coarse standard about earning income by working with a counterpart standard; seed-009's counterpart extends into saving and spending choices, but that extension is not shared ground, so it does not change the concept. Merge candidates for the curation pass.
mergeWhy: The first two are byte-identical twins and the third is the same claim restated at 3-5 ('people earn income by selling their labor'); survivor has the cleanest test, and the third's extra human-capital clause ('skills and experience change what that labor earns') is a genuinely different idea that should be split into its own concept rather than kept as a reason to hold this duplicate open.

title: Income Earned Through Work
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that people earn income by working.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate concept: seed-008 and seed-009 state the same idea and carry identical title and description. Both pair the same coarse standard about earning income by working with a counterpart standard; seed-009's counterpart extends into saving and spending choices, but that extension is not shared ground, so it does not change the concept. Merge candidates for the curation pass.
mergeWhy: The first two are byte-identical twins and the third is the same claim restated at 3-5 ('people earn income by selling their labor'); survivor has the cleanest test, and the third's extra human-capital clause ('skills and experience change what that labor earns') is a genuinely different idea that should be split into its own concept rather than kept as a reason to hold this duplicate open.

title: Physical Geography's Influence on Economic Activity
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how landforms, climate, or natural resources in a place shape the work people do and how they live there.
subject: social_studies

title: Cultural Diversity Within Communities
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to recognize that communities contain diverse cultural groups, including the customs and celebrations that distinguish them.
subject: social_studies

title: Rights and Responsibilities of Citizens
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to name or explain the rights citizens hold and the duties they owe in return as members of a community or nation.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: c-civic-participation-and-responsibility's own curator note concedes it is a bridge whose Georgia member already passes the survivor's test ('the duties they owe in return') and whose Ohio member passes neither cleanly; the survivor is the single idea, has 13 members to 2, and the unmatched participation-pays-off half should be rejected rather than preserved as a duplicate.

title: The Purposes Laws Serve in a Community
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain what laws accomplish for a community — order, security, public services, or the protection of individual rights — rather than merely that laws exist.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: 'Why groups and communities need rules and laws at all' and 'what laws accomplish for a community' are the same question answered at two vocabulary levels, and the K-2 framing is not a separate concept because concepts are grade-agnostic; the survivor's test is stronger because it enumerates the purposes (order, security, public services, protection of rights) and explicitly excludes standards that only assert laws exist (see report: close call).

title: Incentives That Influence Choices and Behavior
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how rewards or penalties — including prices — change what people decide to do.
subject: social_studies

title: Scarcity, Choice, and Opportunity Cost
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that limited resources force people to choose, and that every choice gives up an alternative.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate concept: seed-015 and seed-016 state the same idea and carry identical title and description. Both pair the same coarse scarcity-and-trade-off standard with an opportunity-cost counterpart; seed-016's counterpart applies it to a saving-or-spending choice, which is a context, not a different concept. Merge candidates for the curation pass. Also adjacent to seed-020 and seed-031, which I judged genuinely distinct (cost-benefit weighing, and consequences over time) rather than duplicates — worth a human look.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description; the twin's counterpart merely applies the idea to a saving-or-spending choice, which is a context and not a different concept, and the survivor carries 4 members to 2.

title: Scarcity, Choice, and Opportunity Cost
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that limited resources force people to choose, and that every choice gives up an alternative.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate concept: seed-015 and seed-016 state the same idea and carry identical title and description. Both pair the same coarse scarcity-and-trade-off standard with an opportunity-cost counterpart; seed-016's counterpart applies it to a saving-or-spending choice, which is a context, not a different concept. Merge candidates for the curation pass.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description; the twin's counterpart merely applies the idea to a saving-or-spending choice, which is a context and not a different concept, and the survivor carries 4 members to 2.

title: Interdependence of Producers and Consumers
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how producers and consumers depend on each other.
subject: social_studies

title: The Roles of Producer and Consumer
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to distinguish the role of a producer from that of a consumer, including that one person can occupy both roles.
subject: social_studies

title: Markets Where Buyers and Sellers Exchange
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain what a market is or how goods and services move between buyers and sellers within one, including the role of price.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description; survivor is the original and carries more members (3 vs 2).

title: Weighing Costs and Benefits of Decisions
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to compare the costs of a course of action against its benefits before deciding.
subject: social_studies

title: Grievances and Events That Sparked Revolution
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to trace the taxes, protests, or incidents that turned colonial grievances into a united movement against imperial rule.
subject: social_studies
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to trace the British taxes, acts, protests, and incidents of the 1760s and 1770s — such as the Stamp Act, the Boston Massacre, and the Boston Tea Party — that turned the grievances of the thirteen American colonies into a united movement against British rule.
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'colonial grievances' against 'imperial rule' in the abstract, never naming the British colonies or the 1760s-70s. Texas standards on colonists' grievances against Mexico before 1836 pass this test literally, as would any colonial independence movement, producing the same false 1776/1836 alignment as the declaration concept one step upstream.

title: Course and Outcome of a War for Independence
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the major battles or campaigns of a colonial war for independence and explain why one side prevailed.
subject: social_studies
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the major battles, campaigns, or turning points of the American Revolutionary War of 1775-1783 and explain why the thirteen colonies prevailed over Britain — not the course of some other people's war for independence.
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'a colonial war for independence' in the abstract. The Texas Revolution of 1835-36 (the Alamo, Goliad, San Jacinto) is a colonial war for independence against imperial rule and is taught explicitly in Texas grades 4 and 7, so it passes this test and would be falsely aligned to Ohio and Georgia standards about the American Revolutionary War. Same defect class as the declaration concept and equally live.

title: A Declaration of Independence and Its Ideals
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the writing, purpose, or founding ideals of a declaration of independence, including how it answered tyranny and abuse of power.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: All three are the same concept at two levels of specificity; the survivor names the 1776 document while c-declaration-of-independence-ideals says 'a declaration of independence' in the abstract, which Texas's 1836 Declaration of Independence passes literally and would falsely align — tradeoff: the abstract one is an approved seed carrying 6 reviewer-decided memberships against the survivor's 2, so all 6 must be re-tested against the tighter wording as part of the merge rather than carried over.
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the writing, structure, purpose, or founding ideals of the 1776 Declaration of Independence by which the thirteen American colonies broke from British rule — including how it answered tyranny and the abuse of power — and not a declaration of independence issued by some other people in some other era.
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'a declaration of independence' in the abstract, so the Texas Declaration of Independence of 1836 (Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills grades 4 and 7) passes the test literally and would be falsely aligned to Ohio and Georgia standards about the 1776 document — two unrelated events reported to a teacher as the same content. This is an approved seed carrying 6 memberships, so the false alignments are already live.

title: Fundamental Freedoms Protected by the First Amendment
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify or explain the importance of the freedoms guaranteed by the First Amendment.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description; survivor carries 6 members to the twin's 2, and 'the First Amendment' names its document unambiguously so no rewrite is needed.

title: A Bill of Rights Limits Government Power
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how an enumerated bill of rights constrains what government may do and why such limits were written into a constitution.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: Same concept at two levels of specificity; the survivor names the ten amendments added to the U.S. Constitution in 1791 while the other says 'an enumerated bill of rights' and 'a constitution' in the abstract, which the Texas Bill of Rights (Art. I of the state constitution) and Georgia's state constitution pass literally — tradeoff: the abstract one is an approved seed with 7 memberships against the survivor's 2, so those 7 must be re-tested, and some state-constitution members will legitimately fall out (they belong with c-state-and-local-government-structure).
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how the Bill of Rights — the first ten amendments added to the U.S. Constitution in 1791 — protects individual rights by constraining what the national government may do, and why those limits were written into that document; a standard about a state's own constitutional bill of rights does not belong here.
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'an enumerated bill of rights' constrains 'a constitution' in the abstract. Nearly every state constitution contains a bill of rights — Texas's is Article I of its 1876 constitution and Georgia teaches its own state constitution in SS8CG1 — so a standard about a state's own bill of rights passes this test and would be falsely aligned to standards about the 1791 federal amendments. This is an approved seed carrying 7 memberships.

title: Regional Differences and Rising Sectional Tension
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how differing regional economies, environments, or institutions produced conflicting interests between sections of a nation.
subject: social_studies
note: Genuine mismatch — recommend rejecting the pair. One member is a geography standard about how regions developed from their physical environments and economies; the other is a history standard naming states' rights and slavery as causes of the Civil War. They share a vocabulary (North, South) but not an idea, and the reviewer's own rationale concedes the split. The title above is a bridge invented to hold them together, and it is broad enough that a pure regional-geography standard and a pure Civil-War-causes standard would both attach — exactly the discrimination failure the concept spine is meant to prevent. Reviewer confidence on this pair is 'partial'.
mergeWhy: The sectionalism concept's test ('differing regional economies, environments, or institutions produced conflicting interests between sections of a nation') is a subset of the Civil-War-causes test and admits the same standards, which is precisely the discrimination failure its own curator note calls a 'genuine mismatch' and recommends rejecting; merging it here resolves that flag, but its pure regional-geography members do not belong at the survivor and should be re-homed to c-geography-shapes-economic-activity or dropped rather than carried over.

title: Personal Budgeting of Income, Spending, and Saving
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify the parts of a personal budget or explain why choosing to save rather than spend affects a person's financial well-being.
subject: social_studies

title: Indigenous Cultures Shaped by Regional Environments
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe or compare how Indigenous peoples in different regions developed distinct ways of life from the environments they lived in.
subject: social_studies

title: Migration and Its Impact on Regions
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how the movement of people into or out of a region changes conditions in that region.
subject: social_studies
note: Thin shared ground. One member is a broad geography standard about political, environmental, social, and economic factors moving people, products, and ideas across a hemisphere; the other bundles poverty, drug policy, and migration as forces acting on one region. Only the migration strand of each touches the other, and the second member's other two elements have no home here. Attachment is weak and partial; seed-030 is the clean migration pair against the same coarse standard.

title: Reasons People Migrate and Where They Settle
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain why people leave one place for another and where they come from or settle.
subject: social_studies

title: Present and Future Consequences of Choices
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that a decision made now carries consequences reaching into the future, including what was given up at the time.
subject: social_studies

title: Productive Resources That Make Goods and Services
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to name or describe the categories of resources used to produce goods and services — natural, human, capital, and entrepreneurship.
subject: social_studies
note: Asymmetric grain. One member defines a single productive resource (entrepreneurship) within a four-resource taxonomy; the other is about resource availability driving specialization and trade. The shared ground is only the taxonomy — the specialization-and-trade half of the second member is seed-034's concept, not this one. The title follows the taxonomy, so the second member attaches on a fraction of what it says.

title: Division of Labor and Productive Capacity
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how dividing work into specialized tasks changes how much can be produced or the standard of living.
subject: social_studies

title: Specialization as a Driver of Trade
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how specializing in what a person, region, or country produces best leads to trade and mutual interdependence.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate concept: seed-034 and seed-039 state the same idea and carry identical title and description. They share one member outright and their other members both say specialization produces trade between regions or countries; only the grade differs. Merge candidates for the curation pass.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description across three seeds that differ only in the grade of the paired standard; survivor is the original and ties the most members (3).

title: Trade Between Regions Promotes Economic Activity
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how trade between regions or countries increases economic activity or the goods and services available.
subject: social_studies
note: Half-shared pair. One member's claim is causal (specialization produces interdependence and trade); the other says only that trade promotes economic activity, with no mention of specialization. They meet on the benefit of trade, which is what the title states, so the concept holds — but the driver the first member is actually about lives in seed-034. Worth checking whether this pair adds anything seed-034 does not.

title: Earning Income by Selling One's Labor
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain that people earn income by selling their labor to employers, or that skills and experience change what that labor earns.
subject: social_studies
note: Partial pair. One member is about human capital — knowledge and skills raising earning power; the other is about selling labor to businesses for income. Shared ground is only 'labor produces income,' which is also seed-008's idea at a lower grade. Neither member states the other's substance, and the reviewer noted the skill-building half is unmatched.
mergeWhy: The first two are byte-identical twins and the third is the same claim restated at 3-5 ('people earn income by selling their labor'); survivor has the cleanest test, and the third's extra human-capital clause ('skills and experience change what that labor earns') is a genuinely different idea that should be split into its own concept rather than kept as a reason to hold this duplicate open.

title: Major World Religions and Belief Systems
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify, compare, or trace the spread of major religions and belief systems and their influence on the cultures that hold them.
subject: social_studies
note: Thin shared ground. One member is about diffusion — how agricultural practices, technology, cultural practices, and religions spread and reshaped a hemisphere; the other is a comparative study of the tenets of belief systems originating in one region. They overlap only on the roster of religions each happens to name. A state standard about cultural diffusion with no religious content, and one comparing religious tenets with no diffusion, would both attach. Candidate for rejection, or for narrowing to comparative belief systems if diffusion is a concept elsewhere in the spine.

title: Economic Systems and the Basic Economic Questions
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to address what to produce, how to produce, and for whom to produce, or to compare how different economic systems answer those questions.
subject: social_studies

title: Specialization as a Driver of Trade
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how specializing in what a person, region, or country produces best leads to trade and mutual interdependence.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate concept: seed-034 and seed-039 state the same idea and carry identical title and description. They share one member outright and their other members both say specialization produces trade between regions or countries; only the grade differs. Merge candidates for the curation pass.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description across three seeds that differ only in the grade of the paired standard; survivor is the original and ties the most members (3).

title: Specialization as a Driver of Trade
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how specializing in what a person, region, or country produces best leads to trade and mutual interdependence.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate of existing concept c-specialization-leads-to-trade (and its already-flagged twin c-specialization-leads-to-trade-2). Title and description copied verbatim so the merge is greppable. This seed's shared ground — uneven distribution of productive resources leading to specialization and trade between countries — is exactly what that concept states; the existing pair already carries the same causal claim at grades 5 and 6. Third member of the merge set.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description across three seeds that differ only in the grade of the paired standard; survivor is the original and ties the most members (3).

title: Markets Where Buyers and Sellers Exchange
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain what a market is or how goods and services move between buyers and sellers within one, including the role of price.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate of existing concept c-markets-exchange-goods-and-services; title and description copied verbatim so the merge is greppable. Also asymmetric grain: the grade-7 member is a broad historical claim (the growth of cities and empires fostered markets, and market exchange drove specialization and the shift from barter to monetary economies), of which only 'markets exist and exchange happens in them' is shared with a grade-3 standard about price allocation. That member attaches on a fraction of what it says; its barter-to-money half lives in seed-042.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description; survivor is the original and carries more members (3 vs 2).

title: Currency as the Basis for Exchange in Trade
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how money or an agreed currency system, rather than direct barter, is what makes exchange work — including why parties trading across borders need a means of converting one currency into another.
subject: social_studies
note: Thin, half-shared pair; reviewer confidence 'partial' and I agree. The Ohio member's actual claim is that market exchange in growing cities and empires drove a transition from barter to monetary economies; the Georgia member's is that international trade requires a currency-exchange system. They meet only at 'exchange runs on money rather than barter,' and neither states the other's substance. That shared proposition is also already stated by existing concept c-money-as-medium-of-exchange ('why money works better than direct barter') at K-2 — this may be that concept at a higher grade rather than a new one. Curation should decide between merging into c-money-as-medium-of-exchange and rejecting the pair.
mergeWhy: Both reduce to 'exchange runs on money rather than direct barter' — the survivor states it plainly and c-currency-enables-exchange-in-trade is that same proposition at a higher grade, as its own curator note already suspects; its extra cross-border currency-conversion clause is a genuinely different idea that belongs in its own concept, not as a reason to keep two barter-vs-money tests that both admit the same standards.

title: Motives for European Exploration and Colonization
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the economic, religious, or political reasons Europeans explored and settled lands already inhabited by Indigenous peoples.
subject: social_studies

title: The 1776 Declaration of Independence and Its Ideals
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the writing, structure, purpose, or founding ideals of the 1776 Declaration of Independence by which the thirteen American colonies broke from British rule — including how it answered tyranny and the abuse of power — and not a declaration of independence issued by some other people in some other era.
subject: social_studies
note: Near-duplicate of existing concept c-declaration-of-independence-ideals — recommend merging, but NOT by copying that concept's wording. I deliberately did not reuse its description verbatim: it says 'a declaration of independence' in the abstract, which is the under-specification already flagged in this library, and a state standard about its own 1836-style declaration would pass that test and be wrongly aligned to the 1776 document. The description above names the event. Recommend the existing concept be retitled and rewritten to this wording as part of the merge.
mergeWhy: All three are the same concept at two levels of specificity; the survivor names the 1776 document while c-declaration-of-independence-ideals says 'a declaration of independence' in the abstract, which Texas's 1836 Declaration of Independence passes literally and would falsely align — tradeoff: the abstract one is an approved seed carrying 6 reviewer-decided memberships against the survivor's 2, so all 6 must be re-tested against the tighter wording as part of the merge rather than carried over.

title: The 1776 Declaration of Independence and Its Ideals
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the writing, structure, purpose, or founding ideals of the 1776 Declaration of Independence by which the thirteen American colonies broke from British rule — including how it answered tyranny and the abuse of power — and not a declaration of independence issued by some other people in some other era.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate of seed-044 (identical title and description, verbatim, so the merge is greppable), and a near-duplicate of existing concept c-declaration-of-independence-ideals. Both seeds share the same Ohio member and pair it with a Georgia standard about the same document; seed-044's counterpart interprets the document's three parts at grade 8, seed-045's explains its writing and purpose at grade 4. Grade and emphasis differ; the concept does not. Merge all three into one.
mergeWhy: All three are the same concept at two levels of specificity; the survivor names the 1776 document while c-declaration-of-independence-ideals says 'a declaration of independence' in the abstract, which Texas's 1836 Declaration of Independence passes literally and would falsely align — tradeoff: the abstract one is an approved seed carrying 6 reviewer-decided memberships against the survivor's 2, so all 6 must be re-tested against the tighter wording as part of the merge rather than carried over.

title: From the Articles of Confederation to the Constitution
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify the weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation — the first national government of the United States after independence — and explain how those failures produced the debate over, and the writing and adoption of, the U.S. Constitution in 1787.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: Same concept — weaknesses of the first charter driving the writing and ratification of the Constitution — but here the polarity is inverted from the usual: the ai_draft is the abstract one ('an earlier governing charter', 'a constitutional convention', 'the resulting constitution', which Georgia SS8CG1 and Texas's own state constitutional conventions pass literally) and the seed names the Articles and 1787, so the seed survives on merit despite having fewer members (2 vs 3); the survivor's description should absorb the draft's 'issues debated at the convention' and 'ratification debate' clauses.

title: Causes of the American Civil War
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the issues and events that drove the United States to the Civil War of 1861-1865 — slavery, states' rights and disputes over the nature of federalism, sectional economic differences, the compromises and court decisions of the 1850s, and the secession crisis following the 1860 election.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: The sectionalism concept's test ('differing regional economies, environments, or institutions produced conflicting interests between sections of a nation') is a subset of the Civil-War-causes test and admits the same standards, which is precisely the discrimination failure its own curator note calls a 'genuine mismatch' and recommends rejecting; merging it here resolves that flag, but its pure regional-geography members do not belong at the survivor and should be re-homed to c-geography-shapes-economic-activity or dropped rather than carried over.

title: Causes of the American Civil War
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the issues and events that drove the United States to the Civil War of 1861-1865 — slavery, states' rights and disputes over the nature of federalism, sectional economic differences, the compromises and court decisions of the 1850s, and the secession crisis following the 1860 election.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate of seed-047 (identical title and description, verbatim, so the merge is greppable). Both seeds share the same Ohio member; seed-047 pairs it with a grade-8 enumeration of the war's causes and seed-048 with a grade-4 standard naming states' rights and slavery as the sources of North-South tension. Only grade and grain differ. Note also that existing concept c-regional-differences-and-sectionalism was flagged as a genuine mismatch partly because a Civil-War-causes standard attaches to it; if this concept is adopted, that one loses its history half and should be re-examined or rejected.
mergeWhy: The first two are byte-identical twins and the 6-8 draft states the same causal claim; survivor wins on test quality because it names the 1861-1865 war, the 1850s compromises and court decisions, and the 1860 secession crisis, whereas the draft's 'drove a nation into civil war' is the same abstract defect flagged elsewhere in this library — tradeoff: the draft carries 6 members to the survivor's 2, so the survivor inherits them.

title: Civic Participation and the Responsibilities of Citizens
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain what citizens are responsible for doing as members of a community or nation, or how taking active part in civic and social groups advances shared and personal goals.
subject: social_studies
note: Thin shared ground and an unresolved overlap. The Ohio member is about the payoff of participation (joining civic groups attains individual and public goals); the Georgia member is a bare list of a citizen's responsibilities. They meet only at the general proposition that citizens are expected to act, and the title above is a bridge holding two halves together rather than one idea. Separately, the Georgia member also passes the membership test of existing concept c-citizens-rights-and-responsibilities ('the duties they owe in return'), so this concept and that one will compete for it; the Ohio member passes neither cleanly. Curation should decide whether the participation half is a distinct concept or this pair should be rejected.
mergeWhy: c-civic-participation-and-responsibility's own curator note concedes it is a bridge whose Georgia member already passes the survivor's test ('the duties they owe in return') and whose Ohio member passes neither cleanly; the survivor is the single idea, has 13 members to 2, and the unmatched participation-pays-off half should be rejected rather than preserved as a duplicate.

title: Separation of Powers and Checks and Balances
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how a constitution divides a government into separate branches with distinct powers and lets each branch check the others, whether at the national, state, or local level.
subject: social_studies
mergeWhy: c-constitutional-principles-of-government is an umbrella whose test enumerates separation of powers, checks and balances, and federalism as examples, so every member of c-separation-of-powers-and-checks and c-federalism-national-and-state-powers passes it automatically; the umbrella should be dissolved into those two existing, better-discriminating concepts rather than kept, even though it has 3 members to the survivor's 2 (see report: close call — an alternative is to narrow the umbrella to popular sovereignty and republicanism, which currently have no home).

title: Federalism: Powers Divided Between National and State Governments
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how a federal system distributes authority between a national government and the states or provinces within it, including which powers are delegated, reserved, or shared.
subject: social_studies

title: The U.S. Bill of Rights Limits Government Power
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how the Bill of Rights — the first ten amendments added to the U.S. Constitution in 1791 — protects individual rights by constraining what the national government may do, and why those limits were written into the document.
subject: social_studies
note: Near-duplicate of existing concept c-bill-of-rights-limits-government — recommend merging, but I did not copy its wording verbatim. That description says 'an enumerated bill of rights' and 'a constitution' in the abstract, which is the same under-specification already flagged for the declaration concepts: most states' own constitutions contain a bill of rights, so a state standard about its own document would pass that test literally and be wrongly aligned to standards about the federal one. The description above names the 1791 federal amendments. Recommend the existing concept be rewritten to this wording as part of the merge.
mergeWhy: Same concept at two levels of specificity; the survivor names the ten amendments added to the U.S. Constitution in 1791 while the other says 'an enumerated bill of rights' and 'a constitution' in the abstract, which the Texas Bill of Rights (Art. I of the state constitution) and Georgia's state constitution pass literally — tradeoff: the abstract one is an approved seed with 7 memberships against the survivor's 2, so those 7 must be re-tested, and some state-constitution members will legitimately fall out (they belong with c-state-and-local-government-structure).

title: Fundamental Freedoms Protected by the First Amendment
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify or explain the importance of the freedoms guaranteed by the First Amendment.
subject: social_studies
note: Duplicate of existing concept c-first-amendment-freedoms; title and description copied verbatim so the merge is greppable (no rewrite needed here — 'the First Amendment' names its document unambiguously and does not carry the under-specification problem). But the pair itself is weak and probably adds nothing: the Ohio member says only that the Constitution protects citizens' rights by limiting the powers of government and never mentions the First Amendment, so it attaches on a fraction of what it says and passes seed-052's Bill-of-Rights test just as well. Same Ohio standard as seed-052, paired with a narrower Georgia element. Consider rejecting this pair and letting seed-052 carry that member.
mergeWhy: Byte-identical title and description; survivor carries 6 members to the twin's 2, and 'the First Amendment' names its document unambiguously so no rewrite is needed.

title: Government Taxation and Provision of Public Goods
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how a government raises revenue through taxes and uses public spending to provide goods and services that markets do not supply on their own.
subject: social_studies
note: Adjacent to existing concept c-government-trade-barriers, which shares this seed's exact Ohio member (governments impact markets via spending, regulations, taxes, and trade barriers) and whose broad description — 'governments act on markets or trade through tools such as tariffs, quotas, embargoes, taxes, or regulation' — would admit this seed's taxation-and-public-goods member too. That concept's existing note already predicted it would have to split into 'government intervention in markets' and 'trade barriers'; this concept is effectively the fiscal half of that split. Curation should reconcile the three at once rather than treating this as independent.

title: Government Trade Barriers and Market Intervention
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how governments act on markets or trade through tools such as tariffs, quotas, embargoes, taxes, or regulation.
subject: social_studies
note: Asymmetric grain — no single title fits both members well. One is broad (government affects markets via spending, regulation, taxes, and trade barriers); the other is narrow (comparing types of trade barriers). Written broadly, as above, the concept will swallow general fiscal and regulatory standards from future states; written narrowly, it drops three-quarters of the first member. This pair probably needs to become two concepts — government intervention in markets, and trade barriers — once more states arrive.

title: Describing Relative Location Using Spatial and Directional Terms
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe where a person, object, or place is in relation to another using spatial or directional language such as near, far, left, right, or the cardinal directions.
subject: social_studies
note: No existing concept addresses spatial language or relative location; c-geography-shapes-economic-activity is about geography's influence on how people live and work, not about locating things. Relative location is a universal primary-grade geography standard and would attract standards from essentially every state.

title: Using Maps and Globes to Locate and Represent Places
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to use, read, or create maps and globes — including their basic elements — in order to locate or represent places.
subject: social_studies
note: The library has no concept about geographic tools at all; the nearest geography concepts are about environment shaping economic activity and about migration. Map and globe skills are a core, recurring K-5 standard in every state's geography strand.
mergeWhy: One map/geographic-tools skill concept proposed once per band — a standard like 'use a grid system to locate a place' passes all three tests at once, which is exactly the double-membership the Duplicates filter exists to catch; the merged description should absorb the named map elements from the 3-5 version and the gather/analyze/report purpose from the 6-8 version (see report: this set is the closest call, because the 6-8 version's graphs-and-charts reach is arguably wider than map skills).

title: Identifying the Physical Characteristics of a Place
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify or describe the physical features of a place, such as landforms, bodies of water, natural resources, or weather.
subject: social_studies
note: c-geography-shapes-economic-activity requires explaining geography's influence on how people work and live; these standards stop at naming the physical features themselves, which is a distinct and earlier demand taught in every state's primary geography strand.

title: Basic Human Needs and the Difference Between Needs and Wants
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify basic human needs such as food, clothing, and shelter, or to distinguish a need from a want.
subject: social_studies
note: The library's economics concepts start at scarcity, goods and services, and income; none names basic needs or the needs/wants distinction, which is the entry-level economics standard in nearly every state's K-1 curriculum.

title: Ways People and Families Meet Their Basic Needs
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the ways people or families meet their basic human needs, including how those ways differ from one family to another.
subject: social_studies
note: Distinct from identifying what the needs are and from the library's production and income concepts: this is about the household-level ways needs get met. It is a recurring K-1 standard across states and no existing concept states it.

title: Authority Figures and Public Officials and Their Roles
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify authority figures or public officials and describe the responsibilities or roles they carry out.
subject: social_studies
note: The library's government concepts cover why rules exist and what laws accomplish, but nothing covers the people who hold authority. Identifying leaders and their roles is a standard K-2 civics topic in every state.

title: Voting and Democratic Procedures for Making Group Decisions
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain or practice voting or other democratic procedures as the means by which a group makes a decision or selects a leader.
subject: social_studies
note: No existing concept covers voting or collective decision procedures; c-citizens-rights-and-responsibilities is about the rights and duties citizens hold, not about the mechanism of deciding. Voting as group decision making is a core K-2 civics standard nationally.

title: Family and Community Traditions and Why They Matter
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe family or community traditions, beliefs, or language and explain why they are important to the people who hold them.
subject: social_studies
note: c-cultural-diversity-in-communities tests for recognizing that a community contains multiple distinct cultural groups; these standards are about the significance of a family's or community's own traditions, which is a different demand and a staple of K-1 culture strands across states.

title: Characteristics and Actions of a Good Citizen
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify the traits or actions that make someone a good citizen — such as honesty, respect for others, responsibility, community service, or participation in governance — including recognizing individuals who exemplify them.
subject: social_studies
note: c-citizens-rights-and-responsibilities tests for naming the rights citizens hold and the duties owed in return; these standards never mention rights and instead enumerate civic dispositions and actions. c-respecting-rights-of-others requires explaining why rights must be respected, which these do not ask. Good-citizenship traits are a distinct, widely taught K-2 civics topic.

title: Using Historical Sources and Artifacts to Study the Past
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify kinds of historical sources or artifacts and explain how they can be used to learn about the past.
subject: social_studies
note: The library contains no historical-inquiry concept of any kind — every concept is content, not method. Learning that photographs, letters, and artifacts are evidence about the past is a recurring primary-grade history standard across states, distinct from generic research or communication skills.
mergeWhy: One historical-source concept proposed three times, once per band, differing only in vocabulary (artifacts/photographs at K-2, primary-vs-secondary at 3-5 and 6-8); the survivor has the cleanest test because the 3-5 version's 'question the reliability' clause collides with c-evaluating-source-credibility-and-bias, which the library keeps as a separate concept — the merged description should absorb the K-2 word 'artifacts' so object-based primary-grade standards still pass.

title: Sequencing Events and Describing Time in the Past and Present
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to place events in order in time, or to use chronological vocabulary and tools such as timelines and calendars to describe when something happened.
subject: social_studies
note: No concept in the library addresses chronology. Sequencing events and using past/present/future vocabulary and timelines is an explicit, near-universal K-2 history standard (Georgia and Ohio both state it directly), so it would attract members from many states.

title: How People Modify the Physical Environment and the Results
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify ways people change the physical environment to suit their purposes, or the consequences that follow from those changes.
subject: social_studies
note: c-geography-shapes-economic-activity runs the other direction — environment shaping people. Human-environment interaction in the people-act-on-environment direction has no concept in the library and is a standard element of every state's geography strand.
mergeWhy: All four are the same human-environment-interaction idea proposed once per grade band by agents who could not see each other; the 6-8 survivor's test is the union of the other three (adapt + deliberately modify + positive and negative consequences), and the 'consequences' split is not a separate concept because two of the other three already name consequences in their own descriptions.

title: How People Adapt To and Modify Their Physical Environment
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe or explain the ways people adapt to the physical environment where they live or deliberately modify it, and the reasons they do so.
subject: social_studies
note: The library's only geography-and-people concept, c-geography-shapes-economic-activity, runs one direction only (environment shapes work). Nothing in the library tests for human adaptation to, or deliberate modification of, the environment — a core human-environment-interaction idea that Ohio, Georgia, and Texas all carry at multiple grades.
mergeWhy: All four are the same human-environment-interaction idea proposed once per grade band by agents who could not see each other; the 6-8 survivor's test is the union of the other three (adapt + deliberately modify + positive and negative consequences), and the 'consequences' split is not a separate concept because two of the other three already name consequences in their own descriptions.

title: Consequences of Human Modification of the Environment
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify or weigh the positive and negative consequences that follow when people modify the physical environment.
subject: social_studies
note: Distinct from the adaptation-and-modification concept above: that one tests for describing what people do and why; this one tests for evaluating what results. Texas separates them into their own elements at three grades, and the evaluative move (positive vs negative consequences) is a different cognitive demand that other states also assess separately.
mergeWhy: All four are the same human-environment-interaction idea proposed once per grade band by agents who could not see each other; the 6-8 survivor's test is the union of the other three (adapt + deliberately modify + positive and negative consequences), and the 'consequences' split is not a separate concept because two of the other three already name consequences in their own descriptions.

title: Applying Map Elements to Locate Places and Interpret Maps
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to apply map elements — title, legend, symbols, scale, grid system, or compass rose and directions — in order to locate places on or create and interpret a map.
subject: social_studies
note: The library carries no geographic-skills concept at all. Map and globe skills are a named, separately assessed strand in Ohio, Georgia, and Texas alike, and the test here is concrete enough (named map elements, applied) not to swallow general geography content.
mergeWhy: One map/geographic-tools skill concept proposed once per band — a standard like 'use a grid system to locate a place' passes all three tests at once, which is exactly the double-membership the Duplicates filter exists to catch; the merged description should absorb the named map elements from the 3-5 version and the gather/analyze/report purpose from the 6-8 version (see report: this set is the closest call, because the 6-8 version's graphs-and-charts reach is arguably wider than map skills).

title: Distinguishing and Using Primary and Secondary Sources
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to distinguish primary from secondary sources and to use, compare, or question the reliability of the information those sources provide.
subject: social_studies
note: The library carries no historical-inquiry concept. The primary/secondary distinction is a named strand in Ohio, Georgia, and Texas at every grade in this band, and the test is specific to source type rather than to generic critical thinking, so it will not swallow the rest of the skills strand.
mergeWhy: One historical-source concept proposed three times, once per band, differing only in vocabulary (artifacts/photographs at K-2, primary-vs-secondary at 3-5 and 6-8); the survivor has the cleanest test because the 3-5 version's 'question the reliability' clause collides with c-evaluating-source-credibility-and-bias, which the library keeps as a separate concept — the merged description should absorb the K-2 word 'artifacts' so object-based primary-grade standards still pass.

title: Supply and Demand Shape Prices and Market Behavior
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how supply and demand determine the price of a good or service or change the behavior of consumers and producers.
subject: social_studies
note: c-markets-exchange-goods-and-services tests for what a market is and how goods move within one, mentioning price only as a role; it does not test for the supply-and-demand mechanism that sets price. Attaching these standards there would be the vocabulary overlap the model warns against. Supply and demand is canonical elementary economics in every state.

title: The Branches of Government and Their Basic Functions
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify the branches of a government and explain the basic function each branch performs.
subject: social_studies
note: The library has concepts for founding documents and for rights, but none for governmental structure. Identifying the three branches and their functions is a fixed feature of elementary civics in every state, and the test is narrow enough not to absorb general 'levels of government' or checks-and-balances standards.

title: Geographic Factors Influence Where People Settle
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how physical geographic factors influence patterns of settlement or the distribution of population across a place.
subject: social_studies
note: c-geography-shapes-economic-activity tests for geography shaping the work people do; c-reasons-people-migrate-and-settle tests for why people leave one place for another. Neither tests for geography determining where population concentrates. Settlement-pattern geography is a standing element in Ohio, Georgia, and Texas.

title: Origins of Representative Government in Colonial Settlements
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify or compare the systems of government colonists established, including early instances of representative self-government as against monarchy.
subject: social_studies
note: The library's colonial concept (c-motives-for-european-colonization) covers only why Europeans came; its founding-document concepts begin at the Revolution. The colonial roots of representative government — compacts and colonial assemblies — is standard fare in Ohio and Georgia elementary US history and has no home in the library.

title: Westward Territorial Expansion of a Nation
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify the acquisitions, expeditions, or ideologies by which a nation expanded its territory westward across a continent.
subject: social_studies
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'a nation expanded its territory westward across a continent' in the abstract. Lower risk than the founding-document defects because no second state's westward expansion appears in the current corpus, but the wording admits any continental expansion (Russian expansion east of the Urals, Canadian westward settlement) and gives the tool no way to reject it. | earlier note: Nothing in the library touches territorial expansion; the closest concepts are about migration (why individuals move) and sectionalism (why regions conflict), neither of which tests for national territorial growth. Louisiana Purchase / Lewis and Clark / Manifest Destiny is a fixed block of elementary US history in Ohio and Georgia. Proposed on one Texas member because the cross-state pull is high, not because Texas coverage is deep — reviewer should weigh that.
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to identify the acquisitions, expeditions, or ideologies by which the United States expanded westward across North America between 1803 and 1853 — such as the Louisiana Purchase, Lewis and Clark, Manifest Destiny, the Oregon Territory, or the Mexican Cession.

title: Aftermath of Civil War and the Reconstruction That Followed
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the effects a civil war and the reconstruction period that followed had on a nation or its regions, including changes made to its constitution.
subject: social_studies
note: c-regional-differences-and-sectionalism covers the causes of sectional conflict only; nothing in the library covers what a civil war left behind. Ohio and Georgia both carry Civil War effects and Reconstruction in this band, and the two Texas members here sit at different grades, which is itself evidence the idea is grade-portable.
mergeWhy: Same concept proposed by the 3-5 and 6-8 agents; the survivor's test is more specific (names the reform programs, lingering problems, and the constitutional amendments) and carries 5 members to 2, though the merged description should still name U.S. Reconstruction (1865-1877) rather than 'a civil war' and 'a nation'.

title: Adapting to and Modifying the Physical Environment
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe how people adapt to or deliberately modify the physical environment, including the positive and negative consequences of those modifications.
subject: social_studies
note: The library has c-geography-shapes-economic-activity for environment-shapes-people, but nothing for the reverse direction — people acting on the environment. This is a distinct, universally taught human-systems idea; Ohio states it in five separate standards across grades 3, 4, 5, 6, and 8 (e.g. 'People have modified the environment throughout history resulting in both positive and negative consequences', 'human activities modify the physical environment'), none of which currently has a home.
mergeWhy: All four are the same human-environment-interaction idea proposed once per grade band by agents who could not see each other; the 6-8 survivor's test is the union of the other three (adapt + deliberately modify + positive and negative consequences), and the 'consequences' split is not a separate concept because two of the other three already name consequences in their own descriptions.

title: Cultural Diffusion Among Societies
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how cultural practices, technologies, or ideas spread from one society to another, what facilitates that spread, or what results from it.
subject: social_studies
note: Cultural diffusion currently has no concept, so Ohio's three diffusion standards (6.SS.8 diffusion of practices/technology/religions; 7.SS.14 trade routes spreading ideas and technology; 7.SS.15 improvements in transportation and communication facilitating cultural diffusion) are unhoused — 6.SS.8 is in fact parked in c-world-religions-and-belief-systems, which the curator note flags as exactly this mis-fit ('one member is about diffusion... candidate for rejection, or for narrowing'). This concept is the clean home for the diffusion strand and is not a duplicate of the religions concept, which tests comparative belief systems.

title: Comparing Forms of Government and Citizen Participation
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to compare or classify forms of government by how power is held, how far that power is limited, or what role citizens play in choosing leaders.
subject: social_studies
note: The library has no concept for comparative government at all, despite this being one of the densest cross-state clusters in the corpus: Ohio 6.SS.10 categorizes governments as monarchies/theocracies/dictatorships/democracies and ties citizens' liberties to limits on governmental authority, and Georgia repeats 'Compare and contrast various forms of government' across five separate standards in grades 6 and 7. The nearest existing concept, c-bill-of-rights-limits-government, tests an enumerated bill of rights and does not reach government typology.

title: Using Primary and Secondary Sources
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to distinguish between, locate, or use primary and secondary sources to acquire information about a topic or event.
subject: social_studies
note: The library contains no inquiry-skills concepts, so Ohio's three primary/secondary source standards (3.SS.2, 4.SS.2, 8.SS.1) have no home. Texas repeats the same standard verbatim in each of grades 6, 7, and 8. This is core and recurring in every state's social studies framework.
mergeWhy: One historical-source concept proposed three times, once per band, differing only in vocabulary (artifacts/photographs at K-2, primary-vs-secondary at 3-5 and 6-8); the survivor has the cleanest test because the 3-5 version's 'question the reliability' clause collides with c-evaluating-source-credibility-and-bias, which the library keeps as a separate concept — the merged description should absorb the K-2 word 'artifacts' so object-based primary-grade standards still pass.

title: Evaluating Sources for Credibility and Bias
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to judge a source's validity, credibility, accuracy, or bias rather than simply to use it.
subject: social_studies
note: Distinct from merely using sources: this is the evaluative move. Ohio 6.SS.9 states it directly ('Sources should be examined for accuracy and credibility') and it currently has no home. Texas repeats it verbatim in grades 6, 7, and 8. Kept separate from using-primary-and-secondary-sources because the tests discriminate different asks.

title: Claims Supported by Evidence and Reasoning
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to formulate, communicate, or defend a claim or position about a social studies topic using evidence and reasoning.
subject: social_studies
note: Evidence-based argument in social studies has no concept, leaving Ohio 6.SS.9 ('used to effectively communicate and defend a claim based on evidence') and Ohio 8.SS.1 ('to present and defend a position') unhoused. Texas repeats the standard verbatim across grades 6, 7, and 8. Core and recurring wherever inquiry frameworks are adopted.

title: Constitutional Principles That Structure Government
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how a constitution structures a government through principles such as separation of powers, checks and balances, federalism, republicanism, or popular sovereignty.
subject: social_studies
note: c-bill-of-rights-limits-government covers only the rights-limiting half of constitutional government; nothing covers the structural principles. Ohio 8.SS.22 ('established a federal republic... separation of powers, and checks and balances') and Georgia SS4CG1 (popular sovereignty, federal system, representative republic), SS4CG3a (checks and balances, separation of powers), and SS8CG1a/b (a state constitution's structure and its relationship to the national one, separation of powers among a state's three branches) all state it and none has a home.
mergeWhy: c-constitutional-principles-of-government is an umbrella whose test enumerates separation of powers, checks and balances, and federalism as examples, so every member of c-separation-of-powers-and-checks and c-federalism-national-and-state-powers passes it automatically; the umbrella should be dissolved into those two existing, better-discriminating concepts rather than kept, even though it has 3 members to the survivor's 2 (see report: close call — an alternative is to narrow the umbrella to popular sovereignty and republicanism, which currently have no home).

title: Creating and Ratifying a National Constitution
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the weaknesses of an earlier governing charter, the issues debated at a constitutional convention, or the debate over ratifying the resulting constitution.
subject: social_studies
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'an earlier governing charter', 'a constitutional convention', and 'the resulting constitution' in the abstract. Georgia SS8CG1 (the state constitution) and Texas's own constitutional conventions of 1836/1876 pass this test literally, so state-founding standards would be falsely aligned to the 1787 federal convention. Same defect class as the declaration and bill-of-rights concepts, but introduced by an ai_draft rather than a seed. | earlier note: Nothing in the library covers the founding-charter transition. Ohio 8.SS.8 states it as a single content statement ('Problems arising under the Articles of Confederation led to debate over the adoption of the U.S. Constitution'), and Georgia states it twice (SS4H2b: weaknesses of the Articles, the Great Compromise, the Three-Fifths Compromise; SS8H3d: how the weaknesses of the Articles led to writing a new federal Constitution). Distinct from c-declaration-of-independence-ideals and from constitutional-principles, which test the finished document rather than its making.
mergeWhy: Same concept — weaknesses of the first charter driving the writing and ratification of the Constitution — but here the polarity is inverted from the usual: the ai_draft is the abstract one ('an earlier governing charter', 'a constitutional convention', 'the resulting constitution', which Georgia SS8CG1 and Texas's own state constitutional conventions pass literally) and the seed names the Articles and 1787, so the seed survives on merit despite having fewer members (2 vs 3); the survivor's description should absorb the draft's 'issues debated at the convention' and 'ratification debate' clauses.
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation, the issues and compromises debated at the 1787 Philadelphia Convention, or the Federalist-Antifederalist debate over ratifying the United States Constitution — not the drafting of a state's own constitution.

title: Reconstruction and Its Effects After Civil War
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the political, economic, or social changes, reform programs, or lingering problems of the postwar Reconstruction period, including the constitutional amendments it produced.
subject: social_studies
note: Reconstruction has no concept, though all three states teach it: Ohio 8.SS.14 ('The Reconstruction period resulted in changes to the U.S. Constitution, an affirmation of federal authority and lingering social and political differences'), Georgia SS4H6 (13th/14th/15th Amendments, Freedmen's Bureau, sharecropping, Jim Crow) and Georgia SS8H6 (Reconstruction's impact on a state, including the amendments and the removal of Black legislators).
mergeWhy: Same concept proposed by the 3-5 and 6-8 agents; the survivor's test is more specific (names the reform programs, lingering problems, and the constitutional amendments) and carries 5 members to 2, though the merged description should still name U.S. Reconstruction (1865-1877) rather than 'a civil war' and 'a nation'.

title: Sectional Disputes That Led to Civil War
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how disputes over slavery, states' rights, or sectional economic interests drove a nation into civil war.
subject: social_studies
note: The nearest existing concept, c-regional-differences-and-sectionalism, is a bridge the curator's own note calls a 'genuine mismatch' and recommends rejecting: it forces a regional-geography standard and a Civil-War-causes standard together and explicitly warns that 'a pure Civil-War-causes standard' attaching to it is the discrimination failure the spine is meant to prevent. This is the clean causal half, not a duplicate: Ohio 8.SS.12 ('Disputes over the nature of federalism, complicated by economic developments... resulted in sectional issues, including slavery, which led to the American Civil War') and Georgia SS8H5a (slavery, states' rights, nullification, Compromise of 1850) and SS4H5b (states' rights and slavery increased tensions between North and South) all state it. If the reviewer rejects the bridge concept, its Civil-War-causes member belongs here.
mergeWhy: The first two are byte-identical twins and the 6-8 draft states the same causal claim; survivor wins on test quality because it names the 1861-1865 war, the 1850s compromises and court decisions, and the 1860 secession crisis, whereas the draft's 'drove a nation into civil war' is the same abstract defect flagged elsewhere in this library — tradeoff: the draft carries 6 members to the survivor's 2, so the survivor inherits them.

title: Forced Removal of Indigenous Peoples From Their Lands
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain the reasons for, and the consequences of, a government forcibly removing Indigenous peoples from their lands.
subject: social_studies
note: No concept covers removal, though it is taught in every state that teaches national expansion. Georgia states it twice (SS8H4d/e: the removal of the Muscogee and the Cherokee, John Ross, Andrew Jackson, Worcester v. Georgia, the Trail of Tears; SS4H3b: the impact of westward expansion on American Indians including the Trail of Tears) and Ohio names it in 8.SS.11 ('the displacement of American Indians'). c-indigenous-cultures-and-environment tests precontact ways of life and does not reach removal.

title: Geographic Factors Shape the Course of Historical Events
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how physical geography — landforms, waterways, climate, weather — shaped the course or outcome of historical events in a place.
subject: social_studies
note: Distinct from c-geography-shapes-economic-activity, which tests geography's effect on the work people do, not on historical events. Ohio 8.SS.15 ('how historic events are shaped by geography'), Georgia SS4G2a (how each force used the physical geography of each battle site) and Georgia SS8G1d ('the importance of water in [a state's] historical development') all state it and none has a home.

title: Structure and Funding of State and Local Government
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the structure and functions of government below the national level, or how state and local governments raise and spend revenue.
subject: social_studies
note: The library's civics concepts stop at rights, laws, and the national constitution; subnational government has no concept. Georgia devotes six standards to it (SS8CG1-CG6: the state constitution, the legislative, executive, and judicial branches, local governments, and — matching directly — 'Describe how state government is funded and how spending decisions are made' and 'Describe how local government is funded'). Every state teaches its own state and local government somewhere.

title: The Slave Trade and the Plantation Economy
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to explain how the slave trade and the forced labor of enslaved Africans developed and shaped a region's economy.
subject: social_studies
note: No concept covers slavery as an economic system, only as a cause of sectional conflict. Ohio 8.SS.4 states it directly ('The practice of race-based slavery led to the forced migration of Africans to the American colonies and contributed to colonial economic development') and it has no home. The transatlantic slave trade and plantation economy are taught in every state's early-national history.

title: Course and Outcome of a Civil War
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the major battles, turning-point events, or significant figures of a civil war and how they shaped its course and outcome.
subject: social_studies
note: UNDER-SPECIFIED: Says 'a civil war' in the abstract, so any civil war a state teaches (the English Civil War in a world-history strand, for instance) passes the test and would be falsely aligned to American Civil War battle-and-figure standards. Lower live risk than the declaration and bill-of-rights defects because all current members are American, but the same latent trap as more states are added — and note its sibling c-causes-of-the-american-civil-war names its war correctly, so the library is inconsistent with itself. | earlier note: The library has c-course-and-outcome-of-independence-war for a war of independence but no counterpart for a civil war, though all three states teach it: Ohio 8.SS.13 ('Key events and significant figures in American history influenced the course and outcome of the Civil War'), Georgia SS4H5c (Fort Sumter, Gettysburg, Appomattox) and SS4H5d (Lincoln, Lee, Grant, Davis), and Georgia SS8H5b (a state's role in the war). Not a duplicate — the independence-war concept tests a colonial war against imperial rule.
suggestedDescription: A standard belongs here if it asks students to describe the major battles, turning-point events, or significant figures of the American Civil War of 1861-1865 — such as Fort Sumter, Antietam, Gettysburg, Appomattox, Lincoln, Lee, Grant, or Davis — and how they shaped its course and outcome.

title: Using Geographic Tools to Analyze Data
description: A standard belongs here if it asks students to create or interpret maps, graphs, or charts in order to gather, analyze, and report information about people, places, and environments.
subject: social_studies
note: No concept covers geographic tools, leaving Ohio 6.SS.3 ('Geographic tools can be used to gather, process and report information about people, places and environments'), Ohio 7.SS.12 (maps used to trace human settlement over time), and Ohio 6.SS.4 (latitude and longitude for absolute location) unhoused. Texas restates it in every grade of the band. Distinct from geography-shapes-historical-events, which tests a causal claim rather than a tool skill.
mergeWhy: One map/geographic-tools skill concept proposed once per band — a standard like 'use a grid system to locate a place' passes all three tests at once, which is exactly the double-membership the Duplicates filter exists to catch; the merged description should absorb the named map elements from the 3-5 version and the gather/analyze/report purpose from the 6-8 version (see report: this set is the closest call, because the 6-8 version's graphs-and-charts reach is arguably wider than map skills).
